# Classification and Diagnosis

## Histiocyte Society Classification Framework

The modern nosology of histiocytic disorders was fundamentally restructured by the Revised Classification of Histiocytoses and Neoplasms of the Macrophage-Dendritic Cell Lineages, published by the Histiocyte Society in 2016 [@Emile2016Revised]. This consensus classification organized the full spectrum of histiocytic disorders into five major groups: (1) the Langerhans cell (L) group, encompassing Langerhans cell histiocytosis and Erdheim-Chester disease; (2) the cutaneous and mucocutaneous (C) group of non-Langerhans cell histiocytoses; (3) the malignant (M) group of histiocytic and dendritic cell neoplasms; (4) the Rosai-Dorfman disease (R) group; and (5) the hemophagocytic lymphohistiocytosis and macrophage activation syndrome (H) group. The placement of HLH and MAS within a unified group reflects the recognition that these conditions share a common immunopathological substrate --- uncontrolled activation of macrophages and cytotoxic T lymphocytes leading to hypercytokinemia and tissue destruction. This classification distinguished HLH/MAS from neoplastic histiocytoses, positioning it as a clinicopathological entity driven by immune dysregulation rather than clonal proliferation [@Emile2016Revised; @Hritier2016BRAF].

## HLH-2004 Diagnostic Criteria

The most widely utilized diagnostic standard for HLH remains the HLH-2004 criteria, originally developed for the pediatric cooperative treatment protocol and subsequently adopted in adult practice [@Bergsten2017Confirmed]. Diagnosis requires either a molecular confirmation consistent with HLH or the fulfillment of at least five of eight clinical and laboratory criteria. These eight criteria, with their specific thresholds, are: (1) fever, defined as a temperature greater than or equal to 38.5 degrees Celsius; (2) splenomegaly on clinical examination or imaging; (3) cytopenias affecting at least two of three lineages in peripheral blood, with hemoglobin below 90 g/L (or below 100 g/L in neonates), platelets below 100 x 10^9^/L, or neutrophils below 1.0 x 10^9^/L; (4) hypertriglyceridemia with fasting triglycerides greater than or equal to 265 mg/dL (3.0 mmol/L) or hypofibrinogenemia with fibrinogen less than or equal to 1.5 g/L; (5) hemophagocytosis demonstrated in bone marrow, spleen, or lymph node biopsy; (6) low or absent natural killer (NK) cell activity; (7) hyperferritinemia with serum ferritin greater than or equal to 500 micrograms per liter; and (8) elevated soluble CD25 (soluble interleukin-2 receptor, sIL-2R) greater than or equal to 2,400 U/mL [@Bergsten2017Confirmed; @Ehl2018Recommendations].

While the HLH-2004 criteria have served as a diagnostic cornerstone for nearly two decades, several limitations have been recognized, particularly in adult populations. The requirement for NK cell activity testing poses a significant practical barrier, as the assay is technically demanding, not standardized across laboratories, and unavailable in many clinical settings. Moreover, the criteria were originally validated in pediatric cohorts with familial HLH, and their sensitivity and specificity in adults with acquired forms of the disease have been debated [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Knaak2020Hemophagocytic].

## HLH-2024 Updated Criteria

A major advance came with the HLH-2024 update, which addressed the longstanding concerns regarding NK cell activity testing by removing it from the diagnostic criteria entirely. The revised framework thus comprises seven criteria, with diagnosis still requiring fulfillment of five. In a validation cohort, the updated criteria demonstrated 99% diagnostic accuracy, confirming that the elimination of the NK cell activity criterion did not compromise diagnostic performance. This modification substantially improved the practical applicability of the criteria, particularly in resource-limited settings and in the acute care environment where rapid diagnostic decisions are essential. The HLH-2024 criteria retain all other original parameters --- fever, splenomegaly, cytopenias, hypertriglyceridemia or hypofibrinogenemia, hemophagocytosis, hyperferritinemia, and elevated sCD25 --- preserving continuity with the established diagnostic framework while enhancing feasibility.

## HScore: A Probability-Based Diagnostic Tool

An alternative and complementary approach to HLH diagnosis is provided by the HScore, a validated scoring system that generates a probability of reactive HLH based on nine clinical, biological, and cytological variables. These nine variables include: (1) known underlying immunosuppression; (2) temperature; (3) organomegaly (hepatomegaly and/or splenomegaly); (4) number of cytopenias; (5) ferritin level; (6) triglyceride level; (7) fibrinogen level; (8) serum aspartate aminotransferase (AST); and (9) hemophagocytosis on bone marrow aspirate. Each variable is weighted and scored, producing a composite score that correlates with diagnostic probability. At the optimal cutoff of 168 to 169 points, the HScore achieves a sensitivity of 93%, specificity of 86%, and overall diagnostic accuracy of 90% [@Knaak2020Hemophagocytic].

The HScore offers several advantages over the categorical HLH-2004 criteria. By providing a continuous probability estimate rather than a binary determination, it allows clinicians to stratify patients along a spectrum of diagnostic likelihood. The inclusion of AST and the weighting of underlying immunosuppression make the HScore particularly relevant for adult patients, in whom hepatic dysfunction and comorbid immunosuppressive conditions are common. Comparative studies in critically ill adults have confirmed that the HScore and HLH-2004 criteria perform comparably, though the HScore may offer better discrimination in borderline cases [@Knaak2020Hemophagocytic; @Rose2019Recommendations].

## EULAR/ACR Criteria for Macrophage Activation Syndrome

For patients with systemic juvenile idiopathic arthritis (sJIA) in whom MAS is suspected, the 2016 EULAR/ACR/PRINTO classification criteria provide a disease-specific diagnostic framework [@Ravelli2016]. These criteria require a serum ferritin level greater than 684 ng/mL as a mandatory entry criterion, combined with at least two of the following four additional features: platelet count less than or equal to 181 x 10^9^/L, AST greater than 48 U/L, triglycerides greater than 156 mg/dL, and fibrinogen less than or equal to 360 mg/dL. Notably, the thresholds for the EULAR/ACR MAS criteria are considerably lower than those of the HLH-2004 criteria, reflecting the fact that MAS develops against a background of active systemic inflammation in which baseline laboratory values are already abnormal. A ferritin level of 684 ng/mL, while markedly elevated in a healthy individual, may represent a relatively modest rise in a patient with active sJIA whose baseline ferritin is already elevated. This calibration to the autoimmune context enhances the sensitivity of the criteria for early detection of MAS, which is critical given the rapidity with which this complication can become life-threatening [@Ravelli2016; @Crayne2019The].

## Soluble Interleukin-2 Receptor as a Diagnostic and Prognostic Biomarker

Among individual laboratory parameters, soluble interleukin-2 receptor (sIL-2R, also designated sCD25) has emerged as one of the most diagnostically valuable biomarkers in HLH. At a threshold of less than 2,400 U/mL, sIL-2R effectively rules out HLH with a sensitivity approaching 100%, making it a powerful negative predictive tool in the evaluation of patients with unexplained cytopenias and hyperferritinemia. Conversely, sIL-2R levels exceeding 10,000 U/mL carry a specificity of 93% for confirming HLH, providing strong positive predictive value at the upper extreme of the distribution [@Rose2019Recommendations].

Beyond its role in diagnosis, the sIL-2R to ferritin ratio has demonstrated significant utility in distinguishing between etiological subtypes of HLH. A ratio of sIL-2R to ferritin greater than 8.6 is suggestive of lymphoma-associated HLH, reflecting the disproportionate T-cell activation relative to macrophage-driven ferritin production that characterizes malignancy-triggered disease. This ratio can guide clinicians toward earlier consideration of occult lymphoma in patients presenting with HLH of unclear etiology, potentially accelerating targeted diagnostic workup including imaging and tissue biopsy [@Rose2019Recommendations].

## Diagnostic Challenges in Adults

The application of diagnostic criteria originally developed for pediatric populations to adult HLH presents several challenges. Adults with HLH more frequently present with hepatic dysfunction and coagulopathy as prominent early features, in contrast to the fever-and-splenomegaly predominant presentation that is typical in children with familial forms. The higher prevalence of comorbid conditions in adults --- including malignancy, chronic infection, autoimmune disease, and immunosuppressive therapy --- complicates the interpretation of laboratory abnormalities that overlap with those of the underlying condition. Ferritin, for example, is elevated in many conditions common in hospitalized adults, reducing its specificity as a standalone marker. Similarly, cytopenias may reflect chemotherapy effect, marrow infiltration by malignancy, or hypersplenism rather than HLH-specific immune-mediated destruction [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Shakoory2016Interleukin].

The diagnostic requirement for bone marrow hemophagocytosis deserves particular scrutiny. While the morphological finding of activated macrophages engulfing hematopoietic elements is pathognomonic in the appropriate clinical context, hemophagocytosis is neither sensitive nor specific for HLH when considered in isolation. It may be absent early in the disease course and can be observed in sepsis, transfusion reactions, and myelodysplastic syndromes. Current expert recommendations therefore emphasize that the absence of hemophagocytosis on a single bone marrow specimen should not exclude the diagnosis when other criteria are fulfilled [@Rose2019Recommendations; @Knaak2020Hemophagocytic].

## Role of Genetic Testing in Adult HLH

Although familial HLH has traditionally been considered a disease of infancy and early childhood, growing evidence supports the role of genetic testing in adult patients. Hypomorphic variants in genes associated with primary HLH --- including PRF1, UNC13D, STX11, STXBP2, and RAB27A --- can result in partial functional defects that remain clinically silent until adulthood, when an infectious, malignant, or autoimmune trigger unmasks the underlying immune dysregulation. Germline mutations in HAVCR2, encoding the TIM-3 immune checkpoint receptor, have been identified as a cause of subcutaneous panniculitis-like T-cell lymphoma with associated hemophagocytic syndrome, illustrating how genetic susceptibility can intersect with specific disease phenotypes [@Gayden2018Germline]. Whole-exome sequencing and targeted gene panels have increasingly identified pathogenic or likely pathogenic variants in adults with recurrent or refractory HLH, fundamentally altering the perception that genetic HLH is exclusively pediatric [@Maffucci2016Genetic]. These findings carry direct therapeutic implications, as patients with identified genetic defects may benefit from hematopoietic stem cell transplantation as definitive treatment.
